codeReview.summary: The POST /api/agent/spawn API is well-implemented with proper parent-child task linking, authentication/authorization, input validation, and error handling. The implementation creates both a TaskRecord (for runtime execution) and WorkItem (for registry tracking), correctly links parent and child, stores constraints in metadata, and begins async execution. All 16 test cases pass, covering success cases, error cases, auth gating, and registry consistency.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker added comprehensive library documentation at .factory/library/spawn-api.md documenting the spawn API endpoint, request/response formats, error codes, and testing approach. This is a good pattern that should be followed for other features.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: File .factory/library/spawn-api.md created with 83 lines covering endpoint details, implementation files, and usage examples

sharedStateObservations.observation: The implementation correctly follows the established pattern of using X-Authenticated-User header for auth context, consistent with other runtime API handlers (HandleTaskSubmission, HandleCancel, etc.)
sharedStateObservations.evidence: internal/runtime/api.go: HandleSpawn uses authenticateUser() helper, same as HandleTaskSubmission and HandleCancel

sharedStateObservations.observation: The worker followed the go-worker skill's TDD approach - tests were written first (api_spawn_test.go) before implementation (api.go, runtime.go)
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff transcript shows test file creation before implementation edits

summary: The scheduler-spawn-api feature is fully implemented and validated. The POST /api/agent/spawn endpoint accepts parent_id, objective, and constraints; validates parent existence; creates child tasks with proper parent-child linking; stores constraints in metadata; returns 202 with task details; and integrates correctly with the work registry. All 16 test cases pass covering happy path, error cases (400, 401, 404, 405), auth gating, multiple children from same parent, and work item/task consistency. The worker followed established patterns, wrote comprehensive tests first, and added helpful library documentation.
